title: Getting Started with Next.js 14
content:
# Getting Started with Next.js 14

Next.js 14 brings exciting new features and improvements that make building modern web applications faster and more efficient.

## Why Next.js?

Next.js has become the go-to framework for React developers because it provides:

- Server-side rendering (SSR)
- Static site generation (SSG)
- API routes for backend functionality
- Built-in optimization
- File-based routing

## Key Features

### 1. App Router
The new App Router provides a more intuitive way to organize your application structure.

### 2. Server Components
Build faster, more secure applications with Server Components that execute only on the server.

### 3. Built-in Optimizations
Automatic image optimization, font optimization, and script optimization.

## Getting Started

To create a new Next.js project, run:

```bash
npx create-next-app@latest my-app
cd my-app
npm run dev
```

Your development server will start at http://localhost:3000

## Conclusion

Next.js 14 is a powerful framework for building production-grade React applications. Whether you're building a simple blog or a complex web application, Next.js provides the tools you need to succeed.
excerpt: Learn how to get started with Next.js 14 and leverage its powerful features for building modern web applications.
author: Nana Nandintsetseg

title: CAB201 – Programming Principles
content:
# CAB201: Programming Principles – Building Strong OOP Foundations

This was one of my first deep dives into proper software engineering at QUT.

## What the unit covered
- Core OOP concepts: classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation, abstraction
- Java fundamentals: collections, generics, exceptions, streams
- Design principles: SOLID, DRY, KISS
- Basic design patterns (Factory, Singleton basics)
- Unit testing with JUnit

## Key assignments & projects
I built several medium-sized console applications, including:
- A task management system with user authentication simulation
- An inventory management program using inheritance hierarchies

These forced me to think about code organisation, reusability, and testability from the beginning.

## What I learned & personal takeaways
Before this unit I wrote code that "worked". After CAB201 I started writing code that other developers (and future me) could understand and extend easily.  
Learning to apply SOLID principles changed how I approach almost every programming task now.

This unit laid the groundwork for later software development and team project units.
excerpt: CAB201 at QUT taught me the core principles of object-oriented programming in Java, focusing on clean, maintainable, and scalable code design.
author: Nana Nandintsetseg

title: The Complete Beginner’s Map to Computer Science: From Binary to the Internet
content:
# The Complete Beginner’s Map to Computer Science

Computer science can seem incredibly complex from the outside. We use apps, websites, and AI every day, but very few people understand what actually happens inside a computer.

At its core, a computer is simply a machine that processes electricity through tiny switches to perform calculations. Everything we use today software, websites, games, and artificial intelligence is built on top of that simple idea.

This article explains the 
conceptual map of computer science
from the smallest hardware components to the internet and modern software systems.



## Level 1: How Computers Think

Inside every computer is a Central Processing Unit (CPU). The CPU contains billions of microscopic switches called transistors.

Each transistor has two possible states:

- On → 1  
- Off → 0  

These are called bits.

### Bits and Bytes

A bit is the smallest unit of data and represents a 0 or 1.

Eight bits form a byte, which can represent 256 possible values. These values can represent numbers, letters, colours, or instructions.

### Binary and Hexadecimal

Computers use binary, a number system based on powers of two.

Example:

Binary: 1011  
Decimal: 11

Because binary numbers become very long, programmers often use hexadecimal, which uses digits 0–9 and letters A–F.

### Logic Gates

Transistors combine to form logic gates such as:

- AND  
- OR  
- NOT  

These gates perform logical operations and form the basis of Boolean algebra, allowing computers to make decisions and perform calculations.

### Character Encoding

Computers store text using encoding systems like ASCII, which assigns numbers to characters.

Example:

A = 65  
B = 66  

When you type a letter on a keyboard, the computer actually stores the corresponding number.



## Level 2: Hardware and Memory

For a computer to run programs, it needs both processing power and memory.

### Machine Code

The CPU only understands machine code, which is written entirely in binary instructions.

Modern software hides this complexity by using programming languages that eventually get translated into machine code.

### CPU Execution Cycle

The CPU processes instructions through a cycle:

1. Fetch instruction from memory  
2. Decode what the instruction means  
3. Execute the instruction  
4. Store the result  

Modern processors perform billions of these cycles every second.

### Multi-Core Processors

Most CPUs now have multiple cores. Each core can process instructions independently, allowing computers to run multiple tasks simultaneously.

### RAM

Random Access Memory (RAM) stores the data and programs currently being used by the CPU.

RAM acts like a workspace where the computer keeps everything it needs for active tasks.



## Level 3: Programming Languages

Writing machine code directly would be extremely difficult, so developers use programming languages.

Common languages include:

- Python
- JavaScript
- C
- Java
- Go

These languages allow developers to write readable instructions that are later translated into machine code.

### Compilers vs Interpreters

Programming languages are translated in two main ways.

Compiler

- Translates the entire program before execution
- Produces faster programs

Examples: C, C++, Go

Interpreter

- Translates code line by line during execution

Examples: Python, JavaScript

### Variables and Data Types

Programs store information using variables.

Examples of data types include:

- Integers (whole numbers)
- Floating point numbers (decimals)
- Strings (text)
- Booleans (true or false)

### Memory and Pointers

In lower-level languages, variables exist at specific memory addresses.

A pointer stores the address of another variable. This allows programs to manipulate memory directly but also introduces risks such as memory leaks or segmentation faults.



## Level 4: Data Structures

Data structures define how information is organised in memory.

### Arrays

Arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations.

Advantages:
- Fast access using an index

Disadvantages:
- Fixed size

### Linked Lists

Linked lists connect elements using pointers to the next element.

Advantages:
- Flexible size

Disadvantages:
- Slower searching

### Stacks

Stacks follow a Last In, First Out structure.

Common uses:
- Undo systems
- Function call management

### Queues

Queues follow a First In, First Out structure.

Common uses:
- Task scheduling
- Printer job management

### Hash Maps

Hash maps store data as key-value pairs and use hash functions to quickly find values.

### Trees and Graphs

Trees organise data hierarchically, starting from a root node.

Graphs represent networks of connected nodes and are used in systems like navigation, social networks, and recommendation engines.



## Level 5: Algorithms

An algorithm is a step-by-step method for solving a problem.

Algorithms are often grouped into reusable components called functions.

### Control Flow

Programs control logic using:

- Conditional statements (if / else)
- Loops (for / while)
- Boolean logic

### Recursion

Recursion occurs when a function calls itself. It must include a base condition to prevent infinite loops.

### Big O Notation

Algorithms are evaluated using Big O complexity, which describes how performance changes as input size increases.

Examples include:

- O(1) – constant time
- O(log n)
- O(n)
- O(n²)



## Level 6: Programming Paradigms

Different programming styles exist for structuring software.

### Imperative Programming

Developers write explicit step-by-step instructions for the computer.

### Declarative Programming

Developers describe what result they want without specifying every step.

Example: SQL queries.

### Object-Oriented Programming

Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) structures software around objects and classes.

Core principles include:

- Encapsulation
- Inheritance
- Polymorphism
- Abstraction

OOP is widely used in large software systems because it improves organisation and code reuse.



## Level 7: The Internet

Modern computing connects billions of devices through the internet.

### Internet vs Web

The Internet is the physical network of cables, routers, and servers.

The World Wide Web is the system of websites accessed through browsers.

### IP Addresses

Every device connected to the internet has an IP address used to identify it.

### DNS

The Domain Name System (DNS) converts human-readable domain names into IP addresses.

### HTTP

Browsers communicate with servers using the HTTP protocol.

Common response codes include:

- 200 – success
- 404 – page not found
- 500 – server error

### Web Development

Modern websites typically use three core technologies:

- HTML for structure
- CSS for visual styling
- JavaScript for interactivity

### APIs

Applications communicate with each other through APIs, often using HTTP methods such as GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE.

### Databases

Most applications store information in databases, which organise data into tables that can be queried using SQL.

### Security

If user input is not properly validated, attackers can exploit vulnerabilities such as SQL injection to manipulate database queries.



## Final Thoughts

Computer science is built in layers.

Hardware provides the foundation, programming languages allow humans to communicate with machines, algorithms solve problems efficiently, and networks connect everything together.

Understanding how these layers interact gives developers the ability to build complex systems from simple applications to global platforms used by millions of people.

For me, learning these foundations changed how I view technology. Instead of seeing computers as mysterious machines, I began to see them as structured systems that follow clear logical rules.
excerpt: A beginner friendly guide to how computers actually work from binary and CPUs to algorithms, programming, and the internet. Learn the full conceptual map of computer science.
author: Nana Nandintsetseg
